raw_transcript.text:
This quick edit tip
is all about drones.
[TENSE MUSIC PLAYING]
[HUMMING]
What's up, man?

They're working you
good out here, huh?

Nothing I don't
want to be doing.

Sometimes when you're
editing, you'll

find that the footage
doesn't necessarily

need a full score
of melodic music

to serve the story
and the emotion.
That just might be too much.

Sometimes it would be better
served with a more understated

ambience, which you can usually
create with a combination
of tones and drones.

Let's begin with the basic
definition of a drone.

A drone is a
sustained sound that
can be pitched higher or lower.

It often establishes
a tonality upon which

the rest of the piece
is built. A drone can

come from a real
instrument, a vocal sound,

or from an electronic
keyboard such as a synthesizer
or a sampler

Let's look at some
common types of drones
that you might use in a cut.

We've chosen three samples to
cover a wide spectrum of moods
and emotions.

First is a sound you're
probably very familiar with,
the low ominous drone.
[LOW, OMINOUS DRONE PLAYING]

This drone is created digitally
using synths and pads.

It's a staple tool in
the editor's arsenal

for creating danger
and tension wherever
those feelings are called for.

It's pretty hard to go
wrong with drones like this

unless, of course,
you overuse them.

Our second common type of
drone is light and peaceful.
Have a listen.
[LIGHT, PEACEFUL DRONE PLAYING]

You can imagine using
a drone like this

whenever you need to create a
hopeful, dreamlike, or ethereal
mood.

Now our third type of drone
exists somewhere in the middle.

It can usher in pretty
much any type of emotion

or gently blend with
any kind of music,

making it much
easier to transition
in and out of sections of a cut.
Have a listen.
[DRONE PLAYING]

This drone happens to have been
created digitally with synths

as well, though it does have
the warm feeling of sustained
string instruments.

It is, for the most part,
pretty light and warm,

though it does
introduce some tension.

The nice thing about
these "middle of the road

sounding" drones is
that they're flexible.

You can use them on
their own or for leading
into a wide variety of music.

The drone might begin
and carry on for as long

as you need it to
before you gently
ease into full score like this.
[DRONE PLAYING]
[ORCHESTRAL MUSIC PLAYING]
That works great, right?

A drone like this
is the perfect tool

to gradually bring on some
music after a dry scene

without the music feeling
like it comes out of nowhere.

It's interesting to note here
that when played by itself,

the drone has a relatively
ambiguous feeling.

And thus, it's able to
transition into an cue,

with this particular cue being
relatively upbeat and cheerful.

But it could just as
easily transition us
into something scary and dark.
[DRONE MUSIC PLAYING]
[HEAVY PERCUSSION MUSIC PLAYING]
Pretty cool, right?

Hey there, for tons more
free editing training,

head over to our website at
filmeditingpro.com/freetraining.

Here you can download
free editing guides along

with high quality
video training courses

created by our team of
professional Hollywood editors.

Our tutorials cover a wide
range of editing topics,

like cutting awesome movie
trailers, editing action

scenes, how to work with
music and sound design,
and a lot more.

All of these free guides
and videos are available

at
filmeditingpro.com/freetraining.
I'll see you next time.
[MUSIC PLAYING]
